summary:
Chapman examines emerging videogame metadata schemas at a moment when games are increasingly entering museums, archives, schools, and libraries, but widely accepted cataloging guidance remains unsettled. The article is valuable for GBLS because it compares schemas created by information professionals, fan communities, and commercial platforms rather than treating library cataloging in isolation. Chapman asks how videogames challenge and expand traditional metadata standards, especially Resource Description and Access (RDA), Library of Congress vocabularies, and genre/form practice.

The literature review situates videogame cataloging between two older problem areas: three-dimensional objects and electronic resources. Physical games and peripherals can resemble realia, toys, or board games; digital games can resemble software, machine-readable data files, or networked electronic resources. Yet videogames often combine both. A cartridge, disk, manual, controller, accessory, online dependency, patch, downloadable add-on, and executable code may all matter. Chapman uses examples such as *Stack-Up*, *Colossal Cave Adventure*, *Undertale*, *Doom*, and *The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time/Master Quest* to show how titles, editions, versions, platforms, carriers, add-ons, and sources of information become difficult in game cataloging.

The method is comparative analysis. Chapman analyzes five schemas or schema-like systems: the Core Metadata Schema for Cataloguing Video Games (CMS) developed by GAMECIP; the University of Washington GAMER Group's Video Game Metadata Schema (VGMS); MobyGames; Steam; and Itch.io. The first two are academic/library-oriented schemas. MobyGames represents a long-running fan/community database with archival ambitions and documented standards. Steam and Itch.io represent commercial digital distribution platforms whose descriptive elements and user-generated tags reveal what game marketplaces treat as useful for discovery. Chapman compares these systems to core RDA elements as interpreted by OLAC's *Best Practices for Cataloguing Video Games Using RDA and MARC21*, and then analyzes their treatment of genre.

The findings show both compatibility and tension with RDA. Many videogame elements can be crosswalked to RDA, but games require more medium-specific granularity. Title can be difficult because packaging, disk labels, menus, title screens, regional releases, alternate titles, abbreviations, colloquial names, and series/franchise naming may conflict. Statements of responsibility do not map cleanly because games are produced by teams, studios, publishers, distributors, voice actors, testers, and other contributors rather than a single authorial figure. Publication is complicated by retail release dates, region, copyright dates, digital updates, developer self-publishing, and changing company names. Edition and version are complicated by commercial editions, software versions, patches, and downloadable content.

Chapman's analysis strongly supports platform as a distinct metadata element. RDA and OLAC can treat platform information through edition and system details, but all of the videogame schemas provide a platform element because platform can define compatibility, gameplay, historical context, and expression-level difference. The article also highlights the need for system requirements, developer, publisher, summary or game description, media format, release/update information, and carrier descriptions that are more precise than generic computer-carrier terms. For GBLS, this matters because patrons and staff need to know not only what a game is called but whether it can be played, on what hardware, with what dependencies, and in what version.

The genre discussion is especially important. Chapman shows that Library of Congress subject headings and genre/form vocabularies remain thin for games, while game websites and fan databases use genre as a major discovery facet. The article separates user-generated genres, narrative genres, visual styles, and gameplay genres. Steam and Itch.io tags can be rich but subjective and unstable. MobyGames and VGMS provide more structured vocabularies that distinguish gameplay from narrative theme, setting, visual presentation, art style, perspective, mood, and related facets. Chapman's main point is that game genre is overloaded: a single label may mix mechanics, narrative, visual style, audience expectations, marketing terms, and player culture. Faceted vocabularies help reduce that overload.

For the larger GBLS project, Chapman extends the metadata strand by showing that game cataloging is both interoperable and distinctive. Libraries can use RDA and MARC structures, but games require enhanced description if catalogs are to support scholarly research, preservation, circulation, discovery, and player advisory. The article also usefully draws attention to gaps. Because many schemas focus on manifestations or expressions, they may underdescribe material-culture elements such as peripheral hardware, manuals, packaging, sound, video, digital file characteristics, color, number of players, connectivity, and ratings. Those details are not decorative; they affect access, compatibility, preservation, and patron decision-making.

Chapman should be used as a synthesis and comparison source rather than as a user study. Its value lies in clarifying how several schema communities are solving similar problems and where standards still need development. For GBLS, the takeaway is practical: game collections need metadata that can communicate across library systems while remaining faithful to games as interactive, technical, visual, narrative, commercial, and community-described objects. That work is ongoing because game genres, platforms, distribution models, and user vocabularies keep changing.

Trials of Metadata: Emerging Schemas for Videogame Cataloguing
Abigail Chapman
Library and Information Studies, University College London, London, UK

ABSTRACT
In the past decade, videogames have grown in cultural ­importance. As they have begun to fill museums, archives, and libraries, the need to catalogue them has become more pressing. However, widely accepted guidelines for cataloguing videogames are still in the process of being developed. Until now, online databases developed by fan communities have led the way in creating systems that effectively capture videogame metadata, yet a few groups have recently sprung up in academia that have developed coherent schemas for implementation in libraries and museums. This study will examine videogame metadata schemas designed by information professionals, fan communities, and commercial websites. These will be compared to each other and be analyzed in their treatment of RDA core elements and genre classification in order to highlight the ways videogames are challenging and expanding traditional metadata standards. These schemas highlight many of the same medium-specific elements absent from RDA, for example, the value of a platform element distinct from system requirements or edition; the equal emphasis on developer and publisher; or the multifaceted nature of videogame genre.

KEYWORDS Metadata; cataloguing; video games; controlled vocabulary; digital preservation

Introduction
In the fifty years since Atari introduced Pong to the American public, videogames have become a medium of art. The Victoria and Albert Museum, the Smithsonian American Art Museum, and the Museum of Modern Art have each curated major exhibitions showcasing the achievements in art and design of games from Pong to Journey. Videogames have become channels for collaborative storytelling in the hands of players who come together in massively multiplayer online games. They have decayed, leading organizations such as Preserving Virtual Worlds and the Internet Archive to develop strategies for the preservation of these sensitive objects. They have become educational tools as both schools and libraries aspire

A. CHAPMAN

to take advantage of technology to gamify learning. As videogames fill museums, archives, schools and libraries, the need to catalogue them adequately becomes ever more pressing. Until now, online databases developed by fan communities have led the way in creating systems that effectively capture videogame metadata for preservation and dissemination; but a few academic schemas have begun to draw level with these fan-developed databases. This study aims to explore the progress being made in videogame cataloguing through a comparative analysis of five schemas, in order to highlight how this new medium is challenging traditional metadata standards such as Resource Description and Access and Library of Congress Subject Headings.

Literature review
Widely-accepted guidance for videogame cataloguing does not exist. What guidance does exist has been influenced by non-book material cataloguing, particularly by practices developed for board games and electronic resources. This section will provide an overview of past efforts to catalogue those materials, many of which continue to influence videogame cataloguing today. While there have been several recently-developed schemas designed to specifically catalogue electronic resources, such as Dublin Core, the focus here will be on Resource Description and Access (RDA) and its predecessor, the second edition of the Anglo-American Cataloguing Rules (AACR2) as they continue to dominate the discourse on Anglo-American cataloguing.
These early years of videogame cataloguing have been largely spent trying to reconcile the physical and digital nature of the new medium. In her survey of videogame cataloguing standards, Greta de Groat notes that the first substantial guidance specific to videogames came in the early 1980s (2015, p. 137). For context, while videogames were being tentatively acknowledged in the library context, they were rapidly dominating the North American market. In fact, in the 1980s, Sega, Nintendo, and Atari each had second- and third-generation consoles on the market, while home computers allowed gamers to programme and execute videogames or play them through physical carriers such as floppy disks. As de Groat points out, Nancy Olson’s 1983 A Manual of AACR2 Examples for Microcomputer Software and Video Games ‘recommended video games be treated as realia’ while acknowledging the need to supplement physical descriptions of games with ‘program statement’ information (2015, p. 137). The dichotomy between physical and digital can be seen in two videogames of the period, Stack-Up (1985), a puzzle game in a ROM cartridge carrier that utilized a R.O.B. accessory included in the Deluxe Set of the

Journal of Library Metadata

65

Nintendo Entertainment System, and Colossal Cave Adventure (1976), a text adventure game coded in FORTRAN and distributed over a predecessor to the Internet.
Stack-Up and its bulky Robotic Operating Buddy accessory (Figure 1) share many characteristics of three-dimensional media, for which guidance was limited at the time. Olson’s AARC2 guidance for realia generally consisted of recording detailed physical descriptions for games and toys while depositing any other relevant information in notes fields (2001, pp. 142-144). In the transition between AARC2 and RDA, guidance for this medium has not substantially changed, but the fine details of recording physical description continue to confuse. For instance, RDA requires that cataloguers record content type and carrier type using a vocabulary which can be hard to grasp, for instance, ‘three-dimensional form’, ‘tactile three-dimensional form’, or ‘three-dimensional moving image’ (Moore, 2014, p. 25). Determining which of several three-dimensional forms a game should be categorized as, in addition to describing supplementary accessories, can be labor-intensive, particularly when the American Library Association (ALA) recommends a detailed recording of object numbers, colors, measurements, and materials (McGrath, 2012, pp. 2-42). Trying to accurately record the content type, size, materials, and extent of a Stack-Up ROM cartridge alone, let alone a companion robotic controller with multiple arm attachments would certainly require ample experience in this branch of cataloguing.
A second challenge is describing those interactive elements unique to games for which no RDA element exists. For instance, Olson points to the usefulness of recording a descriptive summary, or ‘purpose or intent of use’ (2001, p. 146), while Diane Robson et al. note the often-overlooked importance of ‘duration of play, number of players, and age of players’ (2019, p. 208). These are facets which can ‘improve record retrieval and

Figure 1.  Stack-Up cover R.O.B. peripheral hardware (MobyGames, 2014).

66

A. CHAPMAN

provide information to help users determine if the resources described will meet their needs’, although in practice they are hidden within notes fields (Robson et al., 2019, p. 202). As seen with Stack-Up, these concerns also affect videogames which share many aspects with physical board and tabletop games.
Colossal Cave Adventure, on the other hand, clearly falls into the category of what would have been called machine readable data files (MRDF) by librarians in the 1970s and 80s. Formally published software only began to emerge in the 1980s, before which, Amy Weiss remarks, ‘there were no publication standards for MRDFs, and many could have been considered published only in a very general sense of the term’ (2003, 172). This statement applies to games like Colossal Cave Adventure which were initially disseminated rather than formally published (Figure 2). The ‘disembodied nature’ of MRDFs also meant that in early editions of AACR2 describing content was prioritized over describing carriers, to the point that a change in carrier did not constitute a change in edition (Weiss, 2003, p. 173). While software publishing is now well-established and can be easily recorded, determining editions can still prove troublesome. Jerome McDonough et al., who studied FRBR relationships in computer games, question whether changes to the compilers, platform, or coding language of an electronic resource undetectable to the user merit separate editions (2010a, p. 11). Guidance continues to be fluid on this matter; however, the answers will have implications to preservationists and researchers as inconsistent metadata could result in lost or irretrievable resources. The current shift away from releasing games on physical carriers to releasing

Figure 2. Transcript of Colossal Cave Adventure originally executed on a PDP-10 from developer Will Crowther’s compiled code (Jerz, 2007, 46).

Journal of Library Metadata

67

digital-only files ensures that questions of editions will continue to be brought up in the future.
Finally, one common thread that unites three-dimensional media, electronic resource, and videogame cataloguing is the reliance on guidance from specialized groups. In the case of board and tabletop game cataloguing, Robson et al. praise the rich tradition of encouraging ‘local practices, created in response to needs that have not been met by existing cataloguing standards’ (2019, p. 202). In the case of electronic resources, Weiss notes that often ‘a set of rules is issued and immediately superseded because of new developments in technology’ (2003, p. 173). Because of the rapid obsolescence of electronic media, groups such as Online Audiovisual Cataloguers (OLAC) are better positioned to respond quickly to changes, as opposed to organizations like the ALA and the Library of Congress which often take years to make changes to published guidance. Similarly, in the case of videogames, organizations such as GAMECIP and GAMER Group have developed entirely new schemas to address the specific needs of the medium, while OLAC has worked within the confines of RDA and MARC to provide guidance that is immediately useful to videogame cataloguers. Their responses to the needs of videogame cataloguing will be explored below.

Methods
Over the past twenty years, several videogame metadata schemas have been developed in both academic and commercial contexts. Academic schemas are often developed for the intended use of libraries and museums and emphasize facets that facilitate bibliographic integrity and future academic research, while commercial schemas often prioritize facilitating game discovery. However, both provide valuable insight into the present development and future evolution of videogame cataloguing. This study proposes to foreground progress being made in videogame cataloguing while revealing areas where progress should continue to be made. To do so, selected metadata elements will undergo comparative analysis to discover the shared and unique features of these types of schemas. First, schemas will be compared to each other and to eleven core elements of RDA as interpreted by Online Audiovisual Cataloguers (OLAC). Following this, the schemas will be compared according to their treatment of genre.
Despite a growing interest in videogame metadata—particularly creating controlled vocabularies for genre, gameplay, and narrative—few fully-fledged videogame metadata schemas appear to have been proposed or developed. The schemas chosen for the analysis needed to be granular enough to withstand comparison to a schema as comprehensive as RDA; needed to

68

A. CHAPMAN

be developed specifically for the medium of videogames; and needed to be ready to use.
In searching through academic literature, only two schemas stood out as being able to fulfill these criteria: these were the Core Metadata Schema and the Video Game Metadata Schema. Both were developed through studies firmly rooted in library and information science. The Core Metadata Schema for Cataloguing Video Games (CMS) was developed by the Game Metadata Citation Project (GAMECIP), which concluded in 2017. This schema comprising twenty elements emphasized its compatibility with Dublin Core, RDA, and Library of Congress standards such as the Metadata Object Description Schema (MODS) and MARC (Appendix A.1). GAMECIP subsequently provided feedback for the OLAC document Best Practices for Cataloguing Video Games Using RDA and MARC21. The Video Game Metadata Schema (VGMS) has been developed and refined since 2012 by the University of Washington’s Game Research (GAMER) Group led by Jin Ha Lee and was originally intended for use in the Seattle Interactive Media Museum (Lee et al., 2013). While this project is ongoing as of 2020, the latest published version of the schema released in 2017 comprises fifty-five elements divided into nine overarching entities (Appendix A.2). Not only is VGMS the most granular schema presented here, at its core, it also privileges user experience and game discoverability by developing detailed vocabularies for mood, visual style, narrative, and gameplay elements in addition to traditional bibliographic elements.
Because of the lack of academic schemas, other sources were sought to provide insight into creating videogame records. GAMER Group had cited the influence of online videogame databases such as Giant Bomb, All Game Guide, and MobyGames on the development of their genre element. Databases such as these have for decades filled the void left by heritage organizations which have only recently begun to include videogames in their collections, as well as by game developers and publishers who notoriously tend to not archive their materials (Donahue & Kraus, 2012). Many of these databases even have explicit archival ambitions: Home of the Underdogs, for instance, aims to be the Project Gutenberg of PC games (Home of the Underdogs FAQ, 2004). In examining these databases, one was found that not only had a strong archival component but also had documentation akin to a metadata schema. MobyGames is a website self-described as the ‘oldest, largest and most accurate video game database covering over 270 platforms from 1950 to date’ (MobyGames, 2020a). Aside from its scope, MobyGames was chosen because of its clear statement of purpose regarding its collection of metadata: to ‘meticulously catalogue all relevant information—credits, screenshots, formats, and release info—about electronic games (computer, console, and arcade) on a

Journal of Library Metadata

69

game-by-game basis’ and to ‘record computer game information for posterity [and] historical research’ (MobyGames, 2014). New database entries may be added by anyone holding a MobyGames account via a Game Wizard, a series of menus with about forty elements, many of which are limited by a controlled vocabulary accessed through dropdown menus (Appendix A.3). In lieu of documentation defining schema elements, MobyGames has developed a code of standards to which new database entries must adhere, The MobyGames Standards and Practices. While perhaps lacking the academic rigor of CMS or VGMS, the MobyGames schema nonetheless has been developing and evolving since the website began in 1999, making it the longest-serving of the schemas being analyzed. Moreover, elements from MobyGames have been cross-walked into the Internet Archive Software Library and used to catalogue items.
For comparison, two commercial digital or electronic content distribution services have also been examined. Videogame developer Valve’s distribution platform Steam, launched in 2003, currently acts as a digital storefront for over 30,000 games developed by Valve and other third-party publishers (About Steam, 2020). Itch.io, launched a decade later, is a self-described ‘open marketplace for independent digital creators with a focus on independent video games’ (About Itch.io, 2020). While similar in structure to Steam, Itch.io also hosts hybrid media such as interactive fiction. Other websites, including GAME, GameStop, the Apple App Store, and the Google Play Store, were also considered. However, Steam and Itch.io both enable users to add descriptive tags to videogames, a special feature which comes into play when analyzing genre. While neither Steam nor Itch.io provides a publicly-available metadata schema, by examining their websites, elements for both were derived from those recurring on videogame sale pages (see Appendix A.4 and A.5). It is acknowledged that a group of undefined elements does not constitute a schema if one takes into account Zhang and Gourley’s requirement that it should define ‘data elements and the rules governing the use of data elements’ (2009, p. 73). However, examining what elements of videogame metadata commercial websites find useful to provide consumers may illuminate blind spots in the other schemas presented.
Having been selected, these schemas will undergo comparative analysis. First, they will be compared to each other and to ten core elements of RDA. Since what RDA considers to be core depends on the item being catalogued, the following elements were identified by OLAC as core to videogame cataloguing in Best Practices for Cataloguing Video Games Using RDA and MARC21:

• Title • Series statement • Statement of responsibility

70

A. CHAPMAN

• Place of publication • Publisher’s name • Date of publication • Edition • Identifier of a manifestation • Carrier type • Content type • Extent

Elements that fall outside of those considered essential by OLAC, if they are shared by the videogame schemas, will also be examined. Following this, the schemas’ approach to genre will be compared. Except for CMS, whose genre vocabulary has since been removed from the Internet and was not otherwise publicly archived, all the schemas analyzed possess at least one genre or form element for which they have developed their own controlled vocabulary. Particular attention will be paid to genre, not only because it appears to be an important feature across all schemas, but also because the Library of Congress is currently committed to developing and expanding its genre/form vocabulary to include more terminology representing audiovisual media. Throughout both these analyses, the comparisons will be guided by three criteria from Jeffrey Beall’s guidelines for pairing metadata schemas with knowledge organizations. Beall’s criteria have primarily been used to assess digital projects within libraries and many are project-specific, ‘amount of training required’, for instance (Beall, 2007, 28). However, there are three criteria which work well on a broader level and will be used to guide the following analyses (2007, 28):
• ‘Level of connection to content standards’, particularly the level of connection between each schema and RDA;
• ‘Granularity of formats of description’; • ‘Level of community or domain specificity’, particularly schemas’
accommodation of videogame-specific metadata’.

Analysis
RDA core elements
Title and series statements Videogame titles may be simple. The game Undertale, for instance, has a clear title which remains consistent across its physical packaging for several different platforms, as well as across its online presence in the case of digital downloads. Yet more often than not, locating a title can

Journal of Library Metadata

71

Figure 3. Disk titled The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time, which also includes Master Quest (MobyGames, 2003a).
be more problematic. Issues highlighted by OLAC include collections or compilations with no unifying title and parallel titles in other languages and scripts, often Japanese (OLAC, 2018, p. 27). Lee also note that the same game may have different titles depending on the packaging (2013, pp. 113-114), which may have implications for the catalogue record at large in ‘situations where the cataloguer needs to choose their source of information for title and other information’ (OLAC, 2018, p. 6). Because of these issues, having strong, specific guidance for title transcription is essential.
As an example, in the case of The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time and Ocarina of Time Master Quest, several titles compete for cataloguers’ attention. The physical disk is labeled The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time (Figure 3). However, this disk actually contains both a remastered version of Ocarina of Time and an alternate version of the same game with redesigned dungeons, Ocarina of Time Master Quest. The game packaging, manual and menu screen present both titles, and when each game is selected to be played, a game-specific title screen also appears

72

A. CHAPMAN

Figure 4. Menu screen for Ocarina of Time and Ocarina of Time Master Quest showing both titles (MobyGames, 2003b).
(Figures 4 and 5). In addition, this version was released together with The Legend of Zelda: The Wind Waker in a promotion for the Nintendo GameCube console. In the case of the UK edition, the packaging combined all three games yet presented only one title, The Wind Waker.
The first issue that arises is from where to source the information for the title; as seen in the example above, different sources can yield substantially different answers. In the case of moving images, including videogames, RDA calls for transcribing the title from the title screen (2.2.2.3). However, a permanent label on the physical carrier, packaging, or ‘accompanying materials’ is also acceptable (2.2.2.3.1) as are ‘textual content’ or ‘embedded metadata in textual form that contains a title’ for digital-born resources (2.2.2.3.2). Accessing title screens has often proved difficult for cataloguers due to the lack of equipment necessary to play items; this concern is compounded when working with videogames, many of which can only be accessed via one specific console. Tellingly, both CMS and VGMS reject the title screen as a primary information source, while still revealing a shared approach rooted in a knowledge of RDA. CMS suggests ‘the screen, in-game menu, and/or packaging’ as sources, with no order of preference given (De Groat et al., 2015, p. 6). VGMS requires that its Chief Source of Information (CSI) for physical games

Journal of Library Metadata

73

Figure 5. Front packaging for Ocarina of Time and Ocarina of Time Master Quest showing both titles (MobyGames, 2003c).
be the ‘box, manual, disk/cartridge, additional content, game title screen/ credits,’ in that order (Lee et al., 2017, p. vii). For digital games, the CSI is the ‘informational page available on the official website or app store listing’, followed by the title screen or credits (Lee et al., 2017, p. vii). Neither differs substantially from the spirit of RDA, although in both cases the sources listed are of course more medium-specific. MobyGames does not stipulate any information source, perhaps because its crowdsourced cataloguers are not expected to have an item in hand but rather to source information from online sources. Steam and Itch.io each display a single title provided by videogame creators and therefore not at the discretion of a cataloguer. From the broad range of information sources listed by the noncommercial schemas, one can infer, even at this early stage, that videogame metadata is seldom neatly found in one convenient place.

74

A. CHAPMAN

The second issue is how to treat those variant titles found across sources. RDA allows for parallel and variant titles, which OLAC recommends be generously used in cases when titles vary across information sources. In the example of The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time and Master Quest, cataloguers could follow OLAC’s recommendation to record the individual titles ‘as authorized access points…in the order in which they appear in the resource’ (2018, p. 27). CMS also has title and variant title elements similar to RDA, as well as a third standardized title element which is meant for a ‘version in a different language (when it is not considered a different work)’ (De Groat et al., 2015, p. 8). VGMS differs in that there is only a single, repeatable game title element; however, this may be modified by attributes for ‘alternative’, ‘abbreviated’, ‘colloquial’, and ‘local release’ titles (Lee et al., 2017, p. 1).
MobyGames’ approach to titles differs substantially. Language is emphasized above all: the ‘primary title should be the title of the earliest US release (non-working title). If there is no US release then the UK title or an English title should be used. Outside of this it should be the original release title no matter the origin’ (MobyGames, 2019). All other titles are alternative. Therefore Irozuki Tincle no Koi no Balloon Trip (Figure 6), which was never released in English, is listed on MobyGames under its Romanized Japanese title. Its prequel, which received a UK release, is listed by its English title, Freshly-Picked Tingle’s Rosy Rupeeland, with its Romanized Japanese title listed as an alternate (Figure 7). This inversion of the standardized title element found in MARC reflects the AngloAmerican slant of the database and its contributors. Steam and Itch.io have a similar stance toward titles. While the websites’ interface language can be changed, titles continue to appear almost entirely in English, regardless of users’ language preferences.

Figure 6.  Packaging of Rosy Rupeeland’s sequel which was never released in Europe or the United States (MobyGames, 2018).

Journal of Library Metadata

75

Figure 7.  Packaging of Freshly-Picked Tingle’s Rosy Rupeeland, which was released in Europe (MobyGames, 2008a).
Overall, each of the noncommercial schemas provides ample space to accommodate the unreliable treatment of titles inherent to the medium. As seen in Table 1, CMS and VGMS both show a high degree of connectivity to RDA while adapting their title elements to specifically suit videogames. However, VGMS explicitly allows for the inclusion of abbreviated and colloquial titles from the gaming community, which under RDA might only be added in separate notes fields if included at all. These added attributes not only add granularity to the title element, but also reveal GAMER Group’s consideration of a gaming public who might search for informal title terms.
Closely associated with the title element, at least in the context of videogames, is the series element. While, as OLAC has indicated, ‘video games seldom appear in true publishers’ series’ (2018, p. 35), there continues to be much confusion as to whether a franchise should be treated as a series. OLAC recommends keeping the franchise as part of the title element, noting that the franchise name is often included as a fundamental part of the title, as in the case of Need for speed: undercover (2018, pp. 25-26). VGMS circumvents this confusion by defining its series title element differently: what RDA considers to be a franchise name to be included in the title element—Need for Speed, The Legend of Zelda—VGMS moves to its series title element. For those games not explicitly part of a series, but still sharing ‘intellectual property, related data, and content’, VGMS provides
Table 1. Title and series statement crosswalk.

76

A. CHAPMAN

a franchise name element (Lee et al., 2017, p. 20). Using this schema, Freshly-Picked Tingle’s Rosy Rupeeland, whose protagonist appears in several Legend of Zelda games could be linked to the Zelda franchise. Notably, no other schema provides either series or franchise elements. MobyGames allows users to create groups of similar games; however, the application of these groups appears to be fairly arbitrary. The absence of any franchise or equivalent element in CMS and MobyGames is an unexpected oversight in a medium in which franchises feature prominently. While VGMS may be said to have the most granular approach to the series statement, there is surprisingly little to compare it to. However, the franchise element is a welcome addition for linking groups of games not explicitly sharing a series name.

Statement of responsibility
Due to the complexity of the creation process, videogames rarely have a clear statement of responsibility. OLAC recommends that one ‘record only a clear statement of responsibility…not simply a corporate name with no function indicated, particularly if only represented by a logo. Generally, record credits in a 508 note’, further noting that ‘[u]nless there is a clear “by …” statement, it is best to put video game credits in a note’ (2018, p. 29). For example, the Nintendo seal at the bottom of Figure 5 would not be lead to its inclusion as a statement of responsibility.
That this element lacks relevance to videogame cataloguing is evident in that none of the schemas directly engage with it. Instead, all schemas provide a variety of elements meant to record videogame creators. CMS has a generic credits element, as well as specific developer and publisher elements (Table 2), the latter of which will be described in detail in the following section. VGMS provides a single agent element which is defined as of ‘an individual, organization, or group of individuals or organizations responsible for creation, realization, manufacture, marketing, and/or distribution of a video game’ (Lee et al., 2017, p. 24). Instead of a single element, MobyGames provides a controlled list of thirty-nine roles including developer and publisher, but also more videogame-specific roles such as voiceover artist and tester. While none of the elements is required, when available, ‘[a]ll credits should be entered as listed in the manual and/or in-game as close [sic] as possible’ (MobyGames, 2019). In fact, it is worth noting here that the issue of finding a consistent source of information, discussed in the relation to the title element applies here as well, as game credits are often only available either through secondary sources, or unlocked after playing through the game. MobyGames’s granularity here speaks to their desire to be a repository of information useful for videogame research (2014). While Steam has both publisher and developer

77

elements, Itch.io substitutes its developer element with an author element, perhaps to be more inclusive toward the interactive fiction and webcomics also for sale on the website. In practice it is often used for the game developer; nevertheless, this element is perhaps the closest to RDA’s statement of responsibility, which is somewhat ironic considering that software developers are considered contributors rather than creators in RDA (I.3.1).
However, aside from Itch.io, there seems to be no true connectivity between any of the schemas and this RDA element. Rather, the granularity with which creator and contributor elements are described suggests the difficulty in determining a primary creator. Producer and developer elements feature prominently in most of the schemas; however, elements like credits and agent create space to include all those responsible for a videogame’s creation. MobyGames may have the most videogame-specific terms for its multiple contributor elements, but CMS and VGMS perform the same function with their single, overarching elements.
Publication statement Despite the difficulties pinpointing creators responsible for a videogame, determining the publisher is usually fairly straightforward. While other contributors are often hidden within game credits, publisher information usually features prominently on the title screen or game packaging.1 That being said, each schema approaches the production and publication statements slightly differently. The RDA publication statement consists of three core elements: place of publication, publisher’s name, and date of publication. In the case of unpublished materials, the core element is date of production.
VGMS reduces publication information to two elements: retail release date and agent (Table 3). The latter, as noted above, can also be used to credit other categories of creators. CMS also does not take into consideration the place of publication but does provide the elements publication date and copyright date, even though the latter is often sourced from the

former (De Groat et al., 2015, p. 13). As clear publication dates are rare in videogames, CMS and the other noncommercial schemas all actively encourage cataloguers to seek out secondary sources as references, particularly when seeking the full date as opposed to only the year. CMS also recommends that ‘the publisher name may be taken from or linked to a name authority file such as id.loc.gov or viaf.org, or be linked to a URI for that particular name in order to ensure consistency in the form of the name’ (De Groat et al., 2015, p. 11). This concern for standardization is shared by the MobyGames schema, which is not only the most granular of the schemas—elements include release date, release country, and publisher—but also the one with the most controlled vocabulary. Its standards note, ‘Company names should be listed with the right name corresponding to the release date…[and] country/region’, for instance, ‘Activision Publishing, Inc should be the name used for games published after Jun 9, 2000 and not Activision, Inc’ (MobyGames, 2019). This call for precision is reinforced by extensive, interconnected dropdown menus which control the release country and published by elements (Figure 8). MobyGames is also the only schema to offer an element compatible to place of publication. Itch.io and Steam both provide publisher and publication or release date elements. However, Itch.io also includes a unique date updated element, which acknowledges that digital-born games are particularly prone to constant mutability.
The complete lack of country in most schemas is an interesting omission. While it is understandable that digital-born videogames may be increasingly international—indeed, MobyGames recommends these list ‘Worldwide’ as the release country (2019)—many older games would benefit from the added granularity. Publisher elements, on the other hand, show a strong degree of connectivity across schemas. However, as online distributors

like Stream and Itch.io begin to consistently provide a way for videogame developers to bypass publishers, the publisher element itself may become less essential (Gaming Street, 2019). In fact, on both Steam and Itch.io, many indie games already list the developer as the publisher, blurring the lines between the two. This shift in publishing may also explain why the concept of retail release seems to be more meaningful to many schemas that those of publishing or copyright. Retail release date and release date, disassociated from the concept of publication, can be roughly cross-walked to either date of publication, or date of production in the case of unpublished works, as necessity dictates. Overall, while there is an uneasy degree of connectivity across all the schemas, the distinctions in terminology and the need to resort to secondary sources suggest that more work needs to be done to make these elements more medium-specific.

Edition statement
New editions, versions, and updates are frequently encountered in most media. Both physical and digital-born videogames often are re-released as special or limited editions, while patches and updates lead to new versions. De Groat et al. distinguish between the ‘commercial edition and software version’ (2015, p. 10). For instance, Undertale was released as a physical collector’s edition in 2017. However, in the two years that the game was only available digitally, it underwent several patches that lead to at least eight software versions (Undertale FAQ, 2019). As OLAC notes, these changes can easily be recorded in RDA’s repeatable edition element (2018, pp. 29-30). Documenting version information is not just added flavor, but can be of crucial to videogame scholars and preservationists alike. Jerome McDonough et al. cite the example of Doom, a game for which ‘[d]efining a version…involves considerable attention not only to a sequence of patches and versions, but also to combinations of developer-produced software, third-party add-ons and player-developed modifications’ (2010b, p. 42). This has implications both for the gameplay itself and for executing recorded versions of gameplay or ‘replay files’, which rely on using a version of the game that ‘correspond[s] exactly to the version that was played when it was created’ (McDonough et al., 2010b, p. 43).
Although CMS acknowledges the distinction between ‘commercial edition and software version’ both are recorded in a single version element (De Groat et al., 2015, p. 10). VGMS does differentiate between these two, using a version element to record software versions and a local release subtitle element to record commercial editions (Table 4). Individual patches can also be recorded as additional content under the content type element. Considering how version and edition act as coordinates when searching for videogames, the granularity of these elements could be extremely useful

80

Table 4. Edition and system details crosswalk.

to researchers. Neither MobyGames, Steam, nor Itch.io have version or edition elements, but the latter does provide a date updated element already touched on which serves as an insight to the version of the game currently for sale.
However, there are two additional concerns unique to videogames. The first is the issue of additional content, which is material added ‘to an already-existing game and require[s] that the player already have the “core” game in order to be playable’ (Online Audiovisual Cataloguers, 2018, p. 6). These materials—often sold as expansion packs, expansion passes, downloadable content (DLC), or add-ons—should be treated as separate titles rather than versions of an existing title according to OLAC (2018, p. 6). Most schemas use a similar approach. MobyGames lists add-ons as separate titles of the genre ‘DLC/add-on’, while Steam and Itch.io, which both sell add-ons separately to the parent game, treat them as separate items. VGMS considers additional content significant enough to devote an entire entity to it, composed of the elements content name, content type, and version requirements (Lee et al., 2017, p. 24). However, these are part of the parent title rather than a separate title. CMS does not address this issue at all.
The second concern is how to handle platform. Kaltman et al. provide a detailed examination of what constitutes a platform, explaining in brief that platforms can be either hardware or software ‘for which a game is designed and on which it can be represented to a user’ (2016, p. 5). Examples include consoles like Nintendo 64 or operating systems like Microsoft Windows. OLAC acknowledges that ‘versions of games on different platforms often have significantly different gameplay’, and also that providing a platform edition statement provides ‘a way of differentiating one expression of a game from another’ (2018, p. 30). OLAC, then, considers the platform version to be equivalent to edition. However, the platform is also technical information and should be recorded as such under the MARC elements system details note (538) and system details access to computer files (753).
The importance and usefulness of a separate platform element are evident from the fact that all the videogame schemas here described share it. Both CMS and VGMS have separate version and platform elements. Although neither schema goes into much detail in their definitions, the

Journal of Library Metadata

81

examples provided suggest that these elements are always distinct from each other. MobyGames database entries are only differentiated by their platform version, as there is no version element. Along with these distinct platform elements come extensive controlled vocabularies developed for them. GAMECIP developed one for CMS consisting of 127 terms, which VGMS uses as well. MobyGames’s controlled vocabulary can only be found within the Game Wizard but is over twice as long at 286 terms. As platform names often have peculiar spellings and capitalization, vocabularies such as these ones help immensely in ensuring consistent data entry. The two commercial schemas only record videogames on an expression level. While they include platform elements, these list all the platforms for which a videogame is compatible.
While not an RDA core element in itself, platform elements were nonetheless found across all schemas. In fact, the schemas differ from RDA in their strict separation of edition and platform, with preference given to the latter by MobyGames, Steam, and Itch.io. As was the case with some of the publication statement elements, the schemas here rely on videogame terminology adding medium-specificity.

Identifier for the manifestation
The identifier for the manifestation is a unique numerical code that identifies the videogame being catalogued. In the case of books, this number is usually the ISBN; however, videogames have equivalent numbers, including the Universal Product Code (UPC), the European Article Number (EAN), the Publisher number, and platform numbers (OLAC, 2018, pp. 37-39). Of the schemas, only CMS has an identifier element, designed to hold a ‘UPC, ISBN, publisher-supplied number, or other standard identifier’ (De Groat et al., 2015, p. 5). While an earlier version of VGMS included a UPC element (Lee et al., 2013, p. 109), the present version has no equivalent to an identifier element (Table 5). MobyGames, Steam, and Itch.io also lack an equivalent to identifier, although, in the case of the commercial schemas, website-specific identifiers may be recorded on the back-end of the website and are simply not visible to the consumer. The low connectivity across schemas may be due to the variety and inconsistency of possible identifiers, compared to the relative consistency of an ISBN or ISSN in the case of books and journals. The lack of other workarounds from the schemas also suggests that at the moment, there is no strong identifier equivalent in the videogame community.

Carrier type, extent, and content type
RDA uses three core elements—carrier type, extent, and content type—to describe the manifestation being catalogued. OLAC recommends that content type always be recorded as ‘computer program’ and ‘two or three-dimensional moving image’ (2018, p. 55). The carrier type and extent elements mostly describe the physical carrier through a somewhat obscure controlled vocabulary of nine generic terms.2 OLAC notes that these ‘are often not specific enough, nor are they in common usage by patrons’ (2018, p. 42). For instance, a cataloguer new to videogames, or electronic media generally, may not find it immediately obvious whether a Nintendo 64 Game Pak (Figure 9) is a ‘computer chip cartridge’, a ‘computer disk cartridge’, or ‘other’ (3.3.1.3). Extent uses the same vocabulary as carrier type, so that while the number of disks or cartridges may be clarified, the type remains vague.
As all the schemas here analyzed are designed exclusively for videogames, an equivalent element to content type would seem to be redundant. However, CMS does include a content type element within its schema, ‘intended as an identification of the high-level type of content, such as “digital” or, “software”’ (2015, p. 16). All other schemas appear to consider the content type as implicit in the medium. CMS also provides two elements, media format and extent, that are equivalent to their RDA counterparts (Table 6). However, instead of the nine terms provided by RDA, GAMERCIP has created a much more comprehensive vocabulary of fifty-five terms, which also include branded carriers like the Nintendo 64 Game Pak, Atari 2600 cartridge, and Emerson Arcadia 2001 cartridge (GAMECIP, 2020). This vocabulary is shared by VGMS, which subdivides carrier type into a series of related elements: distribution type to distinguish between physical and digital items; physical format to describe

physical game carriers; and file format and file size to describe digital games (Lee et al., 2017, pp. 17-18). There is no exact equivalent to extent, although file size could contain some similar information. Similarly, MobyGames provides a media type element with its own controlled vocabulary of fifty terms, many of which are also branded. As both Steam and Itch.io only provide digital files for download, a carrier type equivalent is not relevant to the websites; however, file size is recorded in both cases under a system requirements element which will be explored further below.
It should also be noted that the schemas use the term media type differently that RDA does. Kaltman et al. define media type or format as ‘any collection of game data encapsulated in a physical medium that requires a computer game platform for presentation to and interpretation by a human being’ (2016, p. 6). In RDA, on the other hand, media type refers to ‘what type of equipment, if any, is needed to use the resource’, which in the case of videogames is always a computer (Online Audiovisual Cataloguers, 2018, p. 39).
One of the reasons RDA’s vocabulary is so poorly suited to videogame cataloguing is that its generic terms cannot reflect the symbiotic relationship between the carrier or media type and the platform. As Kaltman et al. go on to say, ‘a platform, whether hardware or software, is defined through the media formats or software that it can support and not solely through its own technical components or interfaces’ (2016, p. 6). In turn, videogames themselves are defined by both platform and carrier, which together provide both a historical and technical context for them. CMS, VGMS, and the MobyGames schema all reinforce this relationship through their medium-specific carrier type elements and controlled vocabularies which work in tandem with the platform element discussed above.
Additional schema elements Aside from the core RDA elements discussed above, there are a handful of elements that recur across the videogame-specific schemas analyzed here. These are platform, system requirements, summary, developer, and genre (Table 7). Platform has already been mentioned under the edition

Table 7. Non-core elements shared by all videogame schemas.

element. While OLAC recommends that platform information be imbedded within the edition and system details elements (2018, p. 30), all schemas grant platform its own element complete with controlled vocabulary. On a similar note, all schemas share a system requirements element which is distinct from platform. While the console or platform certainly affects gameplay, both hardware and software requirements determine whether a user can access a videogame at all and are therefore perhaps of more consequence in this medium than in others.
Developer is a third element shared by the schemas. Defined as an ‘entity that designs and executes the creation of video games’ (Gaming Street, 2019), the developer often combines the roles of programmer, designer, and author. Developers are known for a defining style or certain genre. While publishers also have strong brand identities, developers are often both distinguished and distinct in their own right. For instance, Square Enix and HAL Laboratory are two major developers known for creating notably different styles of games, the former represented by series like Final Fantasy, the latter by the Kirby franchise. However, as self-publishing becomes more prominent, developer becomes an increasingly useful element, one that applies to all videogames.
Summary or game description is a fourth element shared by four of the schemas. While Steam and Itch.io game summaries are more akin to marketing material, VGMS and MobyGames require their summaries to be original, unbiased, and focused on plot or gameplay (Lee et al., 2017, p. 2; MobyGames, 2019). While describing a game using original language requires research, providing a summary can be particularly useful in a medium still lacking clear genre definitions. Finally, all schemas share a genre element, which will be explored in detail below.
Notably, all these elements can be accommodated in RDA. However, as a ‘format agnostic’ schema, RDA does not define these as core (Online Audiovisual Cataloguers, 2018, p. 8). Schemas such as these are helping highlight what videogame metadata is both helpful and essential to record. For instance, Lee et al. found in a survey of videogame librarians and patrons that developer, platform, and genre elements were deemed as ‘essential’ as title (2016, p. 837). These elements were all subsequently incorporated into VGMS. Particularly when it comes to faceted searching, each of these extra elements provides valuable additional access points.

Genre elements
A truism repeated by the International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions (IFLA) states, ‘Genre/Form terms describe what something “is”, rather than what it is “about”’ (2020). This aboutness is often expressed in catalogue records with the help of Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH), a controlled vocabulary that has been traditionally slow to react to new media. In the case of videogames, as de Groat notes, LCSH was designed to describe books about videogames. The subject heading ‘video games’, for instance, has nearly two hundred narrower terms, nearly all of which are videogame titles.3 Considering that on MobyGames there were nearly ten thousand new games added in 2019 alone, the LCSH list of terms is bound to be arbitrary and inadequate even when only dealing with books about videogames and not videogames themselves. For instance, a player’s guide about Hot Wheels Turbo Racing could have a subject heading, but not a guide about any one of the dozens of Harvest Moon videogames that have been released continually since 1996. Other subject headings that are not medium-specific are not always helpful. For instance, badgers may be useful when describing the badger simulator Shelter, but bandicota would not contribute any meaning to Crash Bandicoot, a franchise of platformers about an animated, anthropomorphic, mutant bandicoot. In contrast, genre is used to describe ‘works of a given type’ (Library of Congress, 2011, p. 15). Due to a number of complex guidelines, however, there are only a handful of usable videogame genre terms available through LCSH (De Groat, 2015, p. 145; Online Audiovisual Cataloguers, 2018, p. 68):
• Video games • Computer adventure games • Computer baseball games • Computer flight games • Computer war games • Computer word games • Educational games • Fantasy games • Simulation games • Video wrestling games

If Steam tried to classify its 30,000 game library using only these terms, users would surely be at a loss. There is hope that the newer Library of Congress Genre/Form Terms (LCFGT) will step in to generate a more robust vocabulary. Colin Bitter and Yuji Tosaka’s recent survey of LCGFT usage indicates that this vocabulary has already become popular among audiovisual cataloguers specifically with its influx of film and music genre

86

A. CHAPMAN

terms (2020, p. 55). However, as of the latest version, computer and videogames continue to be outside its remit.
The usefulness of genre as a videogame facet should not be neglected. In their study of genre in Anglo-American cataloguing, Hur-Li Lee and Lei Zhang highlight ‘the recent popularity of genre as a search parameter in the Web environment’, (2013, p. 909), with websites like Amazon and IMDb using genre as an integral part of their taxonomies (2013, p. 893). Videogame websites already use genre as a key facet. Clarke et al. have pointed to the key role genre plays in marketing videogames, particularly ‘extant genre labels to capitalize on their existing popularity and understanding’ (2017, p. 453). Indeed, from a marketing perspective, there does appear to be a list of genre terms that are routinely used. Below is a chart compiled in June 2020 of the genres used by the seven listed online game retailers (Table 8). While the number of retailers analyzed is far from comprehensive, the list nonetheless clearly shows several unifying genre

terms describing gameplay: action, adventure, role-playing, simulation, sports, and strategy.
Of course, as videogame studies is, relatively speaking, an emerging field, definitions of these genre terms and even the games to which the terms are applied continue to be malleable. Even the term genre itself, as Lee and Zhang rightly point out, ‘needs a formal and rigorous definition… that conceptualizes genre in terms of content, form, and function’ (2013, p. 909). While such an ambitious task is beyond the scope of this study, what can be examined is how the videogame schemas under analysis conceptualize videogame genre through the elements they create to capture it. Their approaches to genre can largely be broken down into four categories: user-generated genres, narrative genres, visual style genres, and gameplay genres.

User-generated genres
Steam and Itch.io offer a unique opportunity to users in allowing them to create and connect multiple descriptive tags to videogames. Li and Zhang characterize this as a ‘user-generated tag feature enabling players to describe and annotate video games based on their own understanding of genres’ (2020, p. 65). For instance, Celeste has been assigned the genres action, adventure, and indie by Steam, and the genre platformer by Itch. io (Celeste, 2020a; Celeste, 2020b). These are broad terms, encompassing many distinct videogame experiences. In the years since its release, however, users have suggested other words and phrases to describe the game. Table 9 lists the game’s current user tags on Steam and Itch.io. Some tags reiterate the websites’ own genre classifications, but others elaborate on other aspects. Windleharth et al. found that user-generated tags described particular elements of gameplay, pacing, visual style, and even the users’ own feelings about the game (2016, pp. 439-441). These latter tags, such as ‘great soundtrack’ and ‘story rich’ are what Windleharth et al. refer to

Table 9. Steam and Itch.io user tags for the videogame Celeste (Celeste, 2020a; Celeste, 2020b).

88

A. CHAPMAN

as ‘evaluative terms’ (2016, p. 434). Together they form a picture more detailed than any single genre term can paint, and generate suggestions for related games to purchase. On Steam, Ori and the Blind Forest shares seventy per cent of tags with Celeste and may be of interest to someone in the market for a cute, story rich platformer. Together these tags contribute to what Jihee Beak and Hope Olson refer to as a ‘user-centred or cognitive approach’ to information retrieval, which ‘emphasize[s] expression of users’ information needs and information seeking behaviors [sic]’ (2011, p. 2). On Steam and Itch.io, users directly contribute to a taxonomy which helps them navigate these websites on their own terms and, interestingly, these terms are consistent across the websites. Steam and Itch.io have roughly fifty per cent of their top three hundred tags in common. Windleharth et al. point to a number of studies on such ‘crowdsourced metadata’ in which users highlight important bibliographic details that cataloguers overlook, or offer alternative, valuable terminology to traditional vocabularies (2016, p. 421). Moreover, tags created for a website as popular as Steam are bound to turn up ‘emergent terms’ that deserve exploration in the ever-evolving study of videogame genres (Windleharth et al., 2016, pp. 431-432). Itch.io even crowdsources tag definitions, although most are not particularly insightful. In fact, Windleharth et al. note that the main challenge of evaluating user tags is interpreting the ‘ambiguous’ definitions of terminology, (2016, pp. 431-432). This subjectivity makes user-generated tags inadequate for use in most library contexts. Even so, they are highlighted here not only because they are one of the key methods of classification on Steam and Itch.io, but also because they have greatly influenced the creation of several genre vocabularies, most recently those developed for the genre elements of VGMS.

Narrative genres
In their facet analysis of videogame genre, Lee et al. found that ‘the metadata element “genre” was heavily overloaded with multiple dimensions of information’ (2014, p. 137). Caught in the complexity its medium, videogame genre often baulks at the crossroads of gameplay, design techniques, artistic styles, and narrative. Of these facets, narrative, is the one most often downplayed. Influenced by scholars such as Geoff King and Tanya Krzywinska, Mark Wolf, and Espen Aarseth, the prevailing view is that interactivity or gameplay should bear the most weight in defining videogame genre, and not without cause. The narratological stance on genre demands that videogames be read as texts, yet many early videogames like Pong do not easily lend themselves to textual examination. In consequence, the narrative facet of genre remains mostly unexplored.

Table 10. MobyGames narrative theme and setting vocabularies (MobyGames, n.d.).

However, as advances in technology and game development have allowed videogames to become an increasingly capable storytelling medium, the usefulness of narrative classification has become apparent. MobyGames offers a narrative theme element using terms similar to those used to classify books or films (Table 10). These terms are defined by types of dialogue, setting, or tropes: the theme ‘comedy’ contains ‘jokes in conversations or one-liners’, for instance, while ‘war’ unsurprisingly features ‘war as a narrative backdrop’ (MobyGames, n.d.). Notably, gameplay is explicitly excluded from the definitions of these themes, as in the case of ‘martial arts’, which states, ‘Unlike the Fighting genre this theme does not denote a specific gameplay mechanic’ (MobyGames, n.d.). This is complemented by a mostly chronological and geographic setting element, with a select few literary genre terms like ‘sci-fi’ and ‘steampunk’ (Table 10).
Based on their last published guidelines, VGMS includes narrative genre as an element meant to ‘describes the type of gameworld and plot, similar to literary genres such as science fiction and fantasy’ (Lee et al., 2017, p. 2). Terms would be selected from a controlled vocabulary of the same name. However, the vocabulary, which only ever consisted of the single term ‘action’, has since been depreciated. Instead, other elements representing narrative devices have been further developed. These are protagonist, setting, theme, and trope. While protagonist is still being developed, together, the remaining elements display a certain similarity to MobyGames’s treatment of narrative, albeit in much more detail: combined, these vocabularies comprise nearly three hundred terms (Table 11).
Lee et al. have noted that narrative analyses of videogame genre ‘rely heavily on literary genre theory or film genre theory’ (2014, p. 127). By shifting the emphasis away from narrative genre and toward separate

However, as Windleharth et al. found in their analysis of Steam tags, ‘Modern literary genres such as “Science Fiction,” “Horror,” and “ActionAdventure” are well represented in the video game medium’ (2016, pp. 426-427). If this trend continues, a successful narrative genre vocabulary will need to rely on some old-fashioned terminology.
Visual genres
Videogames are a visual medium as much as they are an interactive one. Because of this, visual genres or styles feature in MobyGames, VGMS, and Steam and Itch.io user tags. Early work on visual genre was done by Aki Järvinen who created a forward-thinking vocabulary of ‘audiovisual style’ (2002). This vocabulary, Järvinen argues, ‘can be used to name and categorize games from an esthetic perspective – in a similar manner the field of visual arts has been categorized into different historical stylistic periods’ (2002, p. 115). He identified three distinct styles—’photorealism’, ‘caricaturism’, and ‘abstractionism’—which are further modified by to what extent they simulate reality (‘televisualism’) or fantasy (‘illusionism’) (2002).
While Järvinen’s vocabulary has not gained much traction in defining historic videogame styles, it greatly influenced the GAMER Group, whose first endeavor by Donovan et al. featured these terms in its style element (2013, p. 414). Through further study and surveys, these have evolved into a visual style element defined by the three aspects of ‘color’, ‘light’, ‘style’ (Lee et al., 2017, p. 10). However, while researching Steam tags for GAMER Group, Windleharth et al. noticed that visual style terms were describing multiple facets including ‘look’, ‘motifs’, ‘mood or tone’, ‘movement within [a] certain visual frame’, and ‘technical methods used to create a particular look’ (2016, p. 430). In the end, VGMS also takes this composite approach to visual style or genre, adding elements mood, dimension, and point of view, all of which inform the visual experience of

While these two elements perhaps have less impact on film and television, they are an important part of the visual experience for videogames, one that is often inextricably linked to types of gameplay in terms like ‘first-person shooter’ or ‘2D side scroller’. Should LCFGT begin to incorporate videogame vocabulary, the prominence of these elements in VGMS, the MobyGames schema, and Steam user tags is a strong argument for their inclusion.
Gameplay genres Mark Wolf argues that visual style and narrative should not be the only— and by no means the primary—method of classifying games, as these fail to capture ‘the fundamental differences and similarities which are to be found in the player’s experience of the game’ (2000, p. 114). There have been some schemas developed that dissect these experiences into novel facets: Aarseth et al., for instance, described the game experience in elements including ‘playerstructure’, ‘mutability’, and ‘savability’ (2003, pp. 51-52). However, Clarke et al. have critiqued Aarseth’s scheme because ‘these genre labels do not map to the language commonly used by players or the commercial games industry, thus failing to identify and describe classes of games in the ways that players would identify them’ (2015, p. 448). Instead, there are already a handful of established gameplay terms found on online videogame retailers, as seen above (Table 9). Most gameplay genre vocabularies—including Wolf ’s highly-cited genre schema, the VGMS gameplay genre vocabulary, and MobyGames basic genre and

Journal of Library Metadata

93

gameplay genre vocabularies—use existing terms like these and define them according to their ludic elements. VGMS defines gameplay genre as the ‘overall nature of a video game’s interactivity based on its objectives, types of rules, distinctive characteristics, modes of action, and manners of gameplay’ (Lee et al., 2017, p. 2) (Table 14). Their gameplay genre vocabulary comprises fifty-eight terms, most of which were sourced from academic literature like Wolf ’s and gaming websites and databases like MobyGames (Lee et al., 2014, p. 128). The terms are defined by what the game revolves around, focuses, or involves. For instance, social simulation games ‘revolve around the simulation of social life’, while survival games ‘focus on surviving difficult situations’ (GAMER Group, 2016a).
MobyGames approaches gameplay with almost identical terminology but divided into two elements. Basic genre closely reflects the core genre terms used by online retailers, that is, action, adventure, role-playing, simulation, sports, and strategy. Gameplay genre terms elaborate on basic genre terms: for instance, there are four RPG sub-genres represented in gameplay genre (Table 15). In both elements, the terms are defined by what a player does; by focus, objective, or goal; or by gameplay mechanic. For instance, in a dating simulation ‘players must try to date various AI characters’, while in a survival horror game, the ‘goal of these games is usually to escape the hazardous situation’ (MobyGames, n.d.).
The centrality of players’ actions is crucial to defining not only this element but the medium itself. Wolf has argued that what distinguishes videogames from films is ‘the direct and active participation of the audience’ (2000, p. 113). In turn, what defines gameplay genres are the parameters of this participation. Perhaps this is why most academic discourse points to gameplay as being the defining factor of genre for videogames. This view has been reinforced by gamers who overwhelmingly use gameplay user tags to describe games on Steam (Windleharth et al., 2016, p. 424). Moreover, as was the case with narrative genre and visual genre, there are many terms shared within

Discussion
Review
The detail with which these videogame cataloguing schemas have been developed is reflected in both the core bibliographic and the genre elements. Designed for various sectors and uses, these schemas nonetheless have elaborated on many of the same medium-specific elements, for example, the value of a platform element distinct from system requirements or edition; the equal emphasis on developer and publisher; or the multifaceted nature of videogame genre. The schemas have also contributed to developing videogame-specific vocabularies for media type, platform, and genre, which in turn promise to add greater uniformity to videogame catalogue records. As McDonough et al. note ‘the bibliographic format and rules of description employed by the library community already provide support… What is needed is a commitment to providing an enhanced level of description necessary to support scholarly work’ (2010b, p. 35). This need was reiterated by Kaltman et al., who wrote of the need of ‘relieving confusion in library records that describe game software and helping ensure better access to legacy software through authority control and guidelines for game cataloguing and citation’ (2016, p. 2). Through their schemas and vocabulary sets, GAMECIP, GAMER Group, and the contributors to MobyGames have all helped alleviate that confusion. Additionally, despite the added granularity provided by the schemas, many elements can continue to be readily cross-walked to RDA if need be.
That being said, there were also interesting inconsistencies among the schemas. Perhaps because none of the schemas selected specifically focused

Journal of Library Metadata

95

on recording physical items but rather manifestations and expressions of works, there was a notable lack of metadata elements focusing on peripheral hardware, manuals, and packaging. Similar audiovisual metadata elements like sound, video, digital file characteristics, and even color content were also inconsistently represented, with the MobyGames schema being the most granular and CMS the least. Since the material culture of an object can sometimes be overlooked in a library context, it would be interesting to see whether museums would accord this metadata more value. Elements such as number of players, connectivity, and game rating were also not represented across the board although each of these has some impact on whether users can access the videogame. However, these more quotidian elements have also not been overly explored in academic literature. Hopefully, more studies will build on GAMER Group’s user surveys and help expand the understanding of the usefulness of these elements.
Finally, a key takeaway from analyzing these videogame cataloguing schemas is the strength of their more granular, multifaceted approach to metadata elements. This approach is particularly evident in the case of genre. Mark Wolf created his gameplay genres to be ‘used in conjunction with the existing taxonomy of iconographically or thematically based genres’ (2000, p. 115), while Järvinen believed that audiovisual style had the potential to influence a player’s perception of a videogame as much as gameplay and that an exploration into the relationship between audiovisual styles and genre tropes or conventions was much needed (2002, p. 127). In their respective ways, both MobyGames and VGMS took this advice to heart in their combined narrative, visual, and gameplay genre elements. In fact, GAMER Group’s genre elements were explicitly designed to incorporate a flexibility that would ‘help address the formulation of new genre labels with clearly identifiable meanings by reducing the overload of information contained in a single label’ (2017, p. 459). However, this multifaceted approach is just as evident in the itemization of contributor elements and system requirements elements across the noncommercial schemas in particular.

Future work
Speaking of videogame genres, Mark Wolf writes, ‘As narrative games grow more complex and cinematic, iconographic and thematic generic classifications from film will be able to be applied more usefully, but interactivity will always be an important factor in the way the games are experienced’ (2000, p. 114). It is worth reiterating that videogames are a very new and evolving medium. Any discussion about genre is bound to be dated as soon as it is disseminated. No one would expect an early eighteenth-century

96

A. CHAPMAN

bookseller to know what a crime thriller novel is, although prototypes of both crime thrillers and novels were certainly available. To expect videogame scholars or even avid videogame players to have developed fully-fledged vocabularies for narrative genres, gameplay genres, and game mechanics is premature. As new videogames are developed and new generations of users are able to classify them in evolving terms, folksonomies like Steam and Itch.io user-generated tags will be invaluable in eventually defining videogame genres. Even videogames as pieces of technology continue to evolve, with new consoles being released yearly. Weihs has spoken of researching ‘the bibliographic nature of holograms’ in the 1980s, a technology never quite took off (2001, p. 171). Now, interactive virtual reality experiences appear to be leveling up gaming, yet might just as easily prove a red herring to cataloguers. As technology continues to advance and shift in unexpected ways, both videogame scholars and information professionals will have much future work to stay apace with the future.
